speaker: interviewer
text: I'll do that right after the little bars, [? sir ?].

words.text: I'll do that right after the little bars, [? sir ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: So it'll be three changes,

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, actually-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to ask-- I want to ask [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHS] [INTERPOSING VOICES] [INAUDIBLE] [INTERPOSING VOICES] [INAUDIBLE] At last--

words.text: I want to ask-- I want to ask [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is January the 11th, 1995. The survivor's name is Elizabeth Feldmen-de Jong. I'm the interviewer, Edie Kalb. We're in North York, Ontario, Canada, and the language is English. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Could you tell me your name again, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elizabeth Feldman-de Jong.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm born in Holland, Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I would like to know a little bit about your background. Can you tell me how many children were in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were four children, and my mother and my father had four kids. The oldest one was Sophia, and the second was Frederika, and I was the third, as Elizabeth. And then I had a brother, Joseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me a little bit about your background as-- as you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born in-- in 1916 in December. And my both parents were deaf. They were born deaf, and we-- we spoke all of us on our hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sign language?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly on our hands. Yeah, the sign language, so with our parents, we mostly-- they had a little bit of voice, but mostly they were deaf. They both went to school in Groningen. And my mother was in Groningen 18 years. That was like an institution. They slept there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that institution?

speaker: interviewee
text: Groningen was it was the deaf institution. It was in Groningen. I know the name is Guyot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it located?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Groningen. It was in-- in like a province for the Netherlands. And it was a big, big deaf institution-- I talk about long time ago. And the kids went only to-- my mother was for 18 years and my father. And the-- it was actually my mother was six or seven years old. My mother is from a deaf family altogether. Her parents were deaf. Her two brothers were deaf, the whole family. And my father, it was not. My father had like a meningitis and brain-- the-- the-- the-- the membrane of the brain was-- so when he was very-- he was only three or four years old when he was-- he was from a normal family, like speaking. So when my father was three or four years old, he was very sick. And then after the big fever, his hearing was gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my grandmother-- and if-- you know, your hearing is gone after a while, your speech goes too, and you can't correct yourself. So they-- they sent him also to Groningen. My father was 10 years older than my mother, and he proposed to her. And all the kids in Groningen, in the deaf institution, they had to learn-- had to care for the younger-- younger kids who came in. So my mother's two-- two brothers were also in Groningen. The whole family was deaf like them. So they had to go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- so my father helped my mother's brothers. So when my mother was 14 or 15 years old, my father was already 25, and he fell in love with my mother. So he waited seven years until she came out of school and then married her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father learned in-- in-- in the school, you always learned also a-- a trade. My father learned to make furniture, very fine-- like polishing and some things. And when he came back to Amsterdam, he went to the diamond trade. My father was a diamond cutter. Where-- is this where your family lived, in Amsterdam?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, after-- I was born in Amsterdam. All-- all of us-- my sister, my brother, everybody was born in Amsterdam. So after my mother finished school in Groningen, and she came to Amsterdam, married my father, and then, you know, they got four children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you grew up in Amsterdam. You spent your childhood days in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and it was good-- good childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about your childhood? Did you-- did you have an observant Jewish life there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of Jewish-- how did you know you were a Jew? How did you distinguish yourselves as Jews there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- my-- so, my parents were deaf, I told you. And for them to go in a synagogue, so they wouldn't understand it anyway. They knew all the Jewish laws. My mother-- I don't-- I never asked where she got it from. Maybe from her mother. She told us all the stories. See, my mother was a great storyteller, and my grandmother was living in our place, too. My mother's mother was living also in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: With you in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our home. And they had like-- so they always were telling about Joseph and Moses and the little-- and they had an enormous-- they couldn't speak very well, but had an enormous theatrical way to telling things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as a child, I was already very, very-- knew all the stories, actually more the stories than-- than actually the religion. We know we were Jewish, but we never never went in a shul. They were also a little bit socialist. My parents are a socialist. At that time-- In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was young-- don't forget I was born in 1916. And that-- the-- the-- the socialists came and worked-- the people should work eight hours a day, eight hours a day, and the social-- kids should not supposed to go to work. Kids should go to school until they were 14 at that time. And it was only 48 hours a week you're supposed to work, not 80 hours a week. I talk for a long time ago. So my parents were strong believers in-- in a new-- new world. That they should be--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they active in the social-- because of their hearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they-- no they were not active at all. My mother was very well educated, well, after all, they were so many years in Groningen, and she could read and write very well. And you love to read.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of newspapers and circulation materials?

speaker: interviewee
text: Newspapers and books. In that time, they start library-- used to come there. But they are very heavy on kids to be educated, you know? And my mother was a very strong believer in that you should be-- go to school and learn well. And, you know, and she did a beautiful job even when she was deaf.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give us an idea of what a day would be like growing up in your home? What type of activities you would do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to say my father was a diamond cutter, but at that time when I grew up, it was a lot of people were without work. Like now. It was a very, very bad situation. Not much work, and my mother was sewing-- my mother, making money for-- too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she took in work as a seamstress?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was a good seamstress, very well. Who could make fur, who could everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father was a diamond cutter, but many times, there was no work. When the rest-- if the-- the first thing that people don't buy is diamonds in that time. So there was a lot of times no work. So my-- and there was no-- no social assistance at that time. How much was maybe, maybe one guilder or two guilders, nothing. So my mother had to work to keep us going. And-- I-- I can remember when I come down, she was working in the night to make money. My father was helping her. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a very warm family. See, my mother couldn't hear. And was not very well disturbed by other people. Her whole life was her family. Around her brothers, and you know, and what-- she was a member from the deaf society. They-- they visit each other. It was like a place they had play yards and they read and made little play things like stage play. It was all pantomimes, but they-- they were really-- my mother did a lot of those things. How did you learn to sign?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the signing I did automatically, when I saw my father and my mother signing all the time. But speaking, I had two older sisters, but I have one sister who was six years older than I was. And the second was five years older than I was. So I was already quite-- my sisters were always talking. So I-- I knew talking and also sign language you get-- I did it automatically. And I was in a-- in a highchair, I used to tell my mother the bell rings, you know, or-- or the things we had-- all things that we automatically did. And my mother was very warm, very in tune, very close to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do as a family? Did you-- to entertain yourselves as a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you my mother was always-- I told you, my mother was always in-- in a deaf society. And they were very-- all the deaf people came together, and they did things together. They make plays, they make-- played cards, they, you know, and they talked and they made coffee. There was a little coffee shop there. And I really always, always came there. I was really very much involved in the deaf-- deaf society. Though we could hear, but we still-- for me, it didn't make any difference. I was-- like a little thing. I was very talkative when I was young, very talkative. Talked a blue streak. So my mother went to me in the streetcar, and used to say to me, well, you know, if you talk always on your hands, everybody stares, stares at you what you're doing. And then my mother used to say to me before to me say, listen-- they called me [? Bepi ?]. I'm Elizabeth. She said, I don't want you to talk to me when we go in the streetcar. We go out of the streetcar, you talk to me again. In the streetcar, you're quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I only talked with my mother on my hands and big, you know, big signs and things like this. And everybody was staring. So my mother said, don't talk. I-- I-- was no sooner than a minute in the streetcar, I forgot all about it. I didn't know the reason why I couldn't talk to my mother, you know? And then my mother said, shh, shh, you know, don't talk. But I did not listen. For me, it was so normal. It was a natural way of life.

speaker: interviewee
text: A natural way-- and I did that, I did-- still talk to her, and then we came out of the streetcar, everybody would say to my mother, who is deaf, you or the child? And my mother used to say, no. It's me. And they say, oh, better that way. [LAUGHS] You know, [INAUDIBLE] My mother said to me, do not talk to me you know. But I was so-- tell her stories and things-- we were very close and were very-- I had actually a very nice youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very nice youth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aunts and uncles? Large family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No. My mother had only two brothers, and I had also nephews-- two, three nephews, who were deaf, born deaf. But my mother had four kids who could hear, but my mother was-- my father was not deaf born.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- and my father had a big family. My father had about five sisters, I think, and two brothers. But my father was the youngest of family. So I can remember my aunts and uncles that I had to go Friday night visit them, and they-- they were quite well-to-do people, my father's side. They lived in Amsterdam?

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived in Amsterdam, in Antwerp, but mostly in Amsterdam. My father had two sisters, and they were-- they had a big doll factory. My father had porcelain and they did fairly young. They had a big factory and were quite well-to-do. So Fridays, we had some dinner, we had to visit them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember things, that had to get a big [INAUDIBLE] before I-- you know, when we were small and very-- have to behave myself, you know? Things like this I remember from my father. They could hear. They were not deaf. [INAUDIBLE] Can you tell me a little bit about going to school, growing up in Amsterdam?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- when I was, well, six years old, every kids go to school, and I went just to public school, six years, yeah, till I-- till I was-- that's only six years. And the seven and the and eight and the nine it was actually in higher school.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was high school. What-- what type of Jewish community was there at the time in Amsterdam? How large was it? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: There must be a Jewish community, but I have to tell you the truth. I never associated with it. I have to tell another thing. The first time-- it's maybe not nice to say-- I was in a synagogue, it was in Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you'd never had much-- but what about it socially? Maybe not religiously, but did you have-- did your parents socialize with Jewish people or did they socialize outside the Jewish circle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because we-- in Holland, we were quite assimilated. In Holland, the-- the Dutch Jewish people in Holland lived there for a long time. Like for my husband-- he's from Portugal, they came here 1400, 1500, with the Spanish Inquisition, he came in Holland. They were there for 300, 400 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my-- my-- my-- my-- my-- like my-- my grandmother and my great-grandmother, what I can remember, they were all Dutch. They did not-- maybe far, far, far away they came maybe from Russia. I know that my-- my mother's family, they-- they came later. No, actually my husband's-- no. But my mother came here. My-- my grandmother was born here. Here in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Holland. And I think her mother was even from-- was born in-- in-- in Holland. I-- I go-- they go back a long time, and they were quite assimilated, though they knew they were Jewish. But-- and there was also a Jewish district in Holland, but we never lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did not live in the Jewish district?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we did not specially want to live in-- in-- I don't think they did it on purpose. It just happened that way. So I had girlfriends and-- and they were Gentile and Jewish. And it was not a big deal.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was my next question. So you had friends of-- both Gentile and Jewish friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever experience any type of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not when I was a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: None.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I can remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start to first hear about what was happening in Europe with Hitler's rise to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was already 1939. I was-- I got married in 1936. And my husband-- I show you later picture-- my first husband, Max, his father, he was an artist. We have paintings here. He was an artist, a sculpture. And I got married with him in 1936. And so then I moved from Amsterdam to Haamstede. It was a little place out of-- out of Amsterdam, quite a bit out-- Haamstede, very close to the coastline. And we had a nice house there, 10 rooms. And he painted, and I-- I went to work. I was a designer-- then already. I had my diploma for designing, and I went off to work. And we did not know children at all. We did-- actually, my husband was always against it. He didn't want children. So that was till-- till the war came. We heard already about the war when we were grown up. Like, when-- when Hitler came in power, I was not a child. I was 27 years when I went to Auschwitz. So it was not as a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So around the time that Hitler came to power-- did-- what--

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all-- first of-- We knew about talking about it and you know, and-- then the Germans fled Germany, went to Holland. That was 1937, I think, 1938, before-- before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we knew about it, that the Jewish were-- and the-- the Kristallnacht that they went, you know-- we know about the papers and you know, we heard about it. So we were all very shocked. And-- and my brother-- actually my sister and her husband-- they had German like, Jewish people in-- in their house. They took them, but then they visit us, and we know that there were razzias already in Holland and there was-- Did you hear anything about concentration camps or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we knew that there was concentration-- that they-- listen, if the razzias, the people were sent out, you heard about they went to camps and it was very bad. And they talked about killings. But who would believe it?

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't believe it. So there was-- was there any thought ever of trying to run away? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we did try when-- the war started in 1940, when Germany came in and you heard about and first they came to Poland. Then you were already on-- then they came to Holland. They were already on the march then, and we know how much razzias were there for Jewish people. They picked them up, and they sent them away. It was already very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I lived in Haamstede still, and we know that the war started in 1940-- and we-- when I stayed in Haamstede in my garden, I saw-- I heard and saw the people flying over the Rotterdam and that-- that they captured Holland, you know, they came-- the Germans came in Holland. And he made Rotterdam nearly flat. They bombarded. And Holland is very small. It's not a big country. It's populated, but it's not-- small. So the Germans came in, and we were very, very sad for sure, that-- What happened when they came in? What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we know that-- that-- that-- that-- was bombarded-- the bombarded. And-- and the Dutch government, like the queen and her-- her cabinet and everybody, her people, they fled to England. So Holland was basically without, without a head of state, nothing. So when Germans came in Holland, they took over.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they started-- like the laws were already in Germany-- they came to in Holland. Like you had to wear a star, that you have to give your radio in, that you can't go shopping after five o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was also a very, very traumatic experience. First, I told you I was living in Holland, like-- like wild-- you know, wild? We were just like everybody else. We were not singled out that we were Jewish and I was into-- I told you I was in a sport organization. I was in a youth organization. I did a lot of, you know, we were all active in-- in-- in other things-- sports and things like this and sailing and-- Name the youth organization that you belonged to.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- the first one was the-- the ISC. That is the-- the-- the arbiters, like the workers youth center-- center. And it was for working people, like you came together, and we went on walking things, and we-- we did all kinds of things. And music, we played mandolin and guitar, folks.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a social group.

speaker: interviewee
text: Social group. And all were the same. And it was-- there was all Jewish and-- and Gentile. It was not specific Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue with that group after you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the-- No, I did not in the youth organization whenever I went to Haamstede. But we did go swimming at the [INAUDIBLE]. It's also like-- there was a youth sport group that was not the-- the-- the other group. So we were swimming and-- and all kinds of sports we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So back to what happens when-- when things began to happen-- what happened to your-- what was your husband doing prior to that to-- to make a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly he painted. Mostly he made a book-- like layouts, books, things, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And was his-- was his living affected?

speaker: interviewee
text: Painting, no. He painted in the war, too, but he was sensitive about it happened. He was very-- he knew [INAUDIBLE]. He knew-- he was very, very afraid that time that they told the people would get picked up in razzias and things like that. He made a lot of friends in Haamstede. Haamstede was a small place. It was maybe two or three Jewish families. And the most mine friends were Gentile.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they visit each other, and they used to say if you are-- and they liked us. And we liked them. They were friends of ours. They used to say if there happens something to you, then we're gonna help you. We try to-- you know, I know that I-- some people that I could count on if there really was-- there were already people in hiding in 1941, There was big razzias in Holland already. They took them up, and there were a lot of things. So we get this letter from the-- from the German government, the SS actually, that we had to go out of the coastline, and we only could take with us one suitcase each. When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 19-- first came out, they were lost that we couldn't go to-- it was also a very traumatic experience. Can you remember, I swam every day and suddenly big signs outside, Jewish not-- Jewish can't go in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wear the star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, right away, the star. And-- and we couldn't do shopping before five o'clock. We couldn't do the radio. We couldn't go bicycle. We couldn't go anything. So suddenly, I felt isolated. Between all those people, I think gee, I am different. I am not-- I am not [? Bepi ?] anymore. I'm just an outcast.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then the letter came.

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt very bad, yeah. And it was a funny feeling. Well, well, you know, grow up. I was already 25 or 26. And everything is free, and suddenly you come in a-- in a-- in a environment that you-- that just-- that you-- that you're outcast, you know? You're an absolute outcast. And I couldn't believe. It couldn't have happened. I felt very bad, but the people around me were nice. They came in, and they gave me food, and they tried to do things, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we-- suddenly we get a letter from the-- from German-- from the SS and told us you had to go out of the house in 24 hours. I told you I had a big house. It is not mine house, it was-- but it was-- it was a big house where we lived in, big garden. And so suddenly we had to take-- could take out of the house only a suitcase. I knew about it. before they write out the first one. They said, if you go out of your house, you can only take a suitcase with you, and you have to leave everything behind. And then you-- and the Germans came the next day-- the SS-- and took everything out of the house and brought it to Germany. Emptied out your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Emptied out in-- in the next day already. Came with the big tractors and took everything out. So I had neighbors I was very good with, and I had a piano. I had a piano in my house, and I-- I played a little bit. And I said to her, I said-- if you come in-- we go-- we have to go away, maybe tonight you to take the piano. It was a good one, a Steinway. I said, and you take them out and then you-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You thought you were gonna come back so you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. I don't-- we didn't know. We didn't think about anything. We had to take-- we-- my parents lived already in the ghetto. They lived in Amsterdam. They moved them out of the place, and gave them another place to live in the-- they call it the ghetto. It was like a couple streets. They called it the ghetto, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had to move where they lived to another place, and we-- and then the day that we went with-- with two suitcase to the train, maybe half of the town of Haamstede came with us. And they were crying, and felt bad for us, you know? They were good people. And they said, don't-- come to us. We're gonna visit you. We'll try to keep in touch with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we were only two days by my mother in-- in-- in Amsterdam and the Dutch police, who was already-- they were still good. They was not overtaken by the SS's. And we had a lot-- we had also [INAUDIBLE] in Holland, a lot of people, sympathizers, Nazi sympathizers who were police, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came in the night to my mother's place, and they told us that you have to look for a hiding place. The SS came to us and said we wanted you-- they want you to come to the-- to the headquarters from the SS. Well, you took something out of your house. I didn't know what my friends took out of my house. I was by my mother, you know? And the police said to me, you better-- if you have a hiding place and you have some friends, they really mad. You took things out of your house, and I'm sure they-- they pick you up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those Dutch police took us in the night out of my mother's place, and I said, you go there and there are friends of ours. So soon we came in a house, they said right away, they said, oh, yeah. You can stay with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to backtrack a little-- you said something about some false identification papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, when we were still in Haamstede, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when-- when we were still in Haamstede, we had-- we had people who were-- said why don't you take some false papers and maybe you can-- you don't have to-- we had a big star on-- the Jewish star on our passport. And they made us false papers without-- without-- a false name and also no star on it. But I was still afraid to go. Everybody knew me in Haamstede. I was still afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never used those papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never used them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went into hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- and then we-- I went into hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were-- and who hid you?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, we went to our-- the grocery-- the guy from the grocery store who always had a horse and a wagon, who came out and was talking to us, and said-- but he had five kids, and he had another friend. And he said, I have another friend who has no children, and they want to take you in. He said, I'll bring you there. The kids know you. The kids knew me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they hide you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Haarlem.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where? In their home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. It was in their home.

speaker: interviewer
text: In an attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were in an attic. And it was a room in the-- on the-- on the-- on the-- on the top floor. And those people were fantastic. Enormous good people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take money for doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The people were-- while we were there, I think my brother-in-law, who was-- who was married to my oldest-- my oldest sister didn't even want to go in hiding before-- she couldn't hide-- hide before me. She didn't want to go in hiding before all her family was safe. She was very, very conscious about it. My mother and my father should be in hiding and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was with you in this home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sophia. She-- she-- they-- they-- I think they paid some, but very little. But my parents who were still in Amsterdam, the SS came all the time to my parents, and told them, listen, what happened to your daughter and your son-in-law. See, my mother, who couldn't hear, you know, and said, all the time, I don't know where they are. I don't know. So they was-- they were very afraid that they would pick them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? They went into hiding with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in hiding with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was in hiding altogether with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was in-- in hiding place were my father, my mother, my-- my husband, and me. Four, at least four of us in the small attic for year and a half. We didn't go out of the-- you couldn't go on the street. Nobody's supposed to know that the people were-- and it was very danger to hide people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- the people-- continued with their everyday existence while you were in that house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was working, and she was cooking for us and doing things for us. You had to be very careful. There was only one washroom downstairs. My father couldn't hear and my mother. So that nobody came in-- they had to stay in the washroom until we told them they can go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any news of what was going on, how things were progressing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we-- we had-- at that time already, there-- there was people who were-- the [INAUDIBLE] started to work already. Don't forget it was 1942, and there was a lot of things in Holland and razzias and picking up and people that sent--

speaker: interviewer
text: And not only the-- the Jewish people, it was Gentile people who were taken from their home to work camps in Germany, you know? And Holland actually was the first country who with the whole other-- the whole-- they stopped working, the streetcar, the water. They-- they were-- they had a big strike, that they didn't like that they took not only the Jewish, but they did the Germans. There was a big resistance in Holland.
